title: AI Humor Generation: Cognitive, Social and Creative Skills for Effective Humor
research_questions: Does giving AI creative, social, and cognitive skills enable it to generate humorous captions that resonate with a target audience?
For Gen Z Instagram image caption humor, are HumorSkills captions rated funnier than captions from GPT-4o with prompt engineering?
Are HumorSkills captions rated as funny as the top-rated human-written Instagram comments?
Does the HumorSkills approach still outperform GPT-4o on non-target image sources such as camera-roll-style photos and museum art?

datasets_used.name: Instagram humor captioning accounts evaluation images and comments
Museum Art images
datasets_used.size: 30 museum art images: 15 images from the Museum of Modern Art (The MoMA) and 15 images of art from from the Metropolitan Art Museum (The Met)

methods_proposed.name: HumorSkills
methods_proposed.summary: HumorSkills is a multi-step system for Gen Z-style humorous image captioning. It uses GPT-4o for visual detail extraction, visual humor ideation, and narrative/conflict extrapolation, a fine-tuned GPT-3.5 model to generate captions with and without external narratives, and a GPT-4o-based Gen Z humor ranking agent to select the top five captions.

humor_theories.detail: The paper cites benign violation theory to motivate the idea that humor arises when expectations are violated in a surprising but not overly threatening, disturbing, or wrong way.

The paper discusses semantic script theory as describing how a setup leads listeners toward one interpretation while the punchline supports a different interpretation that is also consistent with the facts.

evaluation_methods.detail: Online surveys asked target-audience raters to rate captions from 1 to 5, where 1 was 'not funny', 3 was 'somewhat funny', and 5 was 'very funny'; image order and caption order were randomized and raters were not told which captions were AI-generated.
The target Instagram study used the top five most upvoted Instagram comments for each image as the human-written comparison condition.
Within HumorSkills, a GPT-4o-based Gen Z humor ranking agent ranked 30 generated captions by humor, relatability, and alignment with the image and narrative, then returned the top five.

headline_results.finding: On the target Instagram caption task, HumorSkills captions were rated 2.27 out of 5 and GPT-4o captions were rated 0.213 points lower, a statistically significant difference reported as p<0.0001.
headline_results.dataset: Instagram humor captioning accounts evaluation images and comments

headline_results.finding: On the same target task, top Instagram captions were only 0.08 points higher than HumorSkills on a 5-point scale, with p=0.053, so HumorSkills was not statistically less funny than the top Instagram captions.
headline_results.dataset: Instagram humor captioning accounts evaluation images and comments

headline_results.finding: The regression table for the target Instagram study reports 6015 observations, a HumorSkill intercept of 2.273, a GPT-4o coefficient of -0.213, and a Top5 IG coefficient of 0.078.
headline_results.value: No. Observations: 6015; Intercept 2.273; GPT-4o rating -0.213; Top5 IG rating 0.078
headline_results.dataset: Instagram humor captioning accounts evaluation images and comments

headline_results.finding: On camera-roll-style Flickr images, HumorSkills was rated 0.291 points higher than GPT-4o, reported in text as 2.19 vs. 1.9 out of 5 and p=0.022; Table 2 reports the HumorSkill coefficient as 0.291 with P>|z| of 0.000.

On museum art images, HumorSkills was rated 0.18 points higher than GPT-4o, reported in text as 2.18 vs. 2.00 out of 5 and p=0.023; Table 3 reports the HumorSkill coefficient as 0.175 with P>|z| of 0.000.
headline_results.value: 0.18 points; 2.18 vs. 2.00 out of 5; p=0.023; Table 3 coefficient 0.175; P>|z| 0.000

key_findings: HumorSkills was significantly funnier than GPT-4o with prompt engineering for Gen Z Instagram caption humor.
HumorSkills was rated almost on par with the top five upvoted Instagram comments and was not statistically less funny than them in the study sample.
HumorSkills also outperformed GPT-4o on non-target camera-roll-style Flickr images and museum art images.

The qualitative analysis attributes stronger captions to Gen Z slang and references, narrative extrapolation to relatable conflicts, and visual detail extraction that targets salient oddities in images.
The paper argues that detail observation, analogous social-situation generation, and audience modeling are human-like skills that can improve LLM humor generation.
limitations: The study targets only one form of humor for one audience: Gen Z-style Instagram image captions.
This humor style tends toward absurdities, which the paper says may be easier to generate than humor that must be both logical and surprising.
Caption humor is more well-defined than forms such as standup comedy or popular humor magazine writing because the image supplies the setup and the system only needs to generate a punchline.
The paper states that LLMs would likely struggle to make in-joke humor without inside information about friends, backgrounds, and shared experiences.
The GPT-4o baseline used a simple humor-writing prompt, and the paper notes that better prompts or multiple generations might produce similar results.
The paper did not conduct ablation studies, so it is unclear which HumorSkills components are most helpful individually.
Some generated captions are described as not logical enough to make sense but not illogical enough to be absurd, making them sound like mistakes.
safety_ethics_notes: The discussion notes that AI systems with human-like social skills such as humor could either augment human bonding or disingenuously create human bonding. It connects this concern to AI-generated persuasion, scams, dating-app message suggestions, and the broader need to distinguish genuine from disingenuous communication.

human_samples.population: Target-audience raters recruited as people who identify as Gen Z and like Instagram caption humor; mostly local-university students were expected to fit the demographic.
human_samples.recruitment: Online survey distributed through email announcements to clubs and classes at a local university; advertised for people who identify as "Gen-Z" and who "like Instagram caption humor"; survey took about 30 minutes and users were paid $10.
human_samples.geography: Local university; country/region not reported for participants.
human_samples.observations: 6015 caption ratings in the target Instagram study; raters saw 20 images with 15 captions each, with image order and caption order randomized. This is an observation count, not a participant count.
human_samples.detail: Demographics reported: 5 male, 9 female, 18 declined gender; 14 aged 18-25, 1 aged 25+, 17 declined age group. Ratings used a 1-5 funniness scale where 1 was "not funny", 3 "somewhat funny", and 5 "very funny". Users were not told the caption condition and were not told AI was involved.

human_samples.population: Target-audience raters recruited the same way as the previous study for the camera-roll/Flickr image survey.
human_samples.recruitment: Paper states raters were recruited the same as for the previous study; exact overlap with the first survey was not reported.
human_samples.geography: Local university recruitment implied by the previous-study method; country/region not reported for participants.
human_samples.observations: 4765 ratings for the Flickr camera-roll-style image study; 30 randomly selected Flickr Image Dataset images, with 5 HumorSkills captions and 5 GPT-4o captions per image. This is an observation count, not a participant count.
human_samples.detail: Demographics reported: 10 female, 4 male, 7 declined gender; 12 aged 18-25, 2 aged 25+, 7 declined age group. Image and caption order were randomized. The paper does not explicitly restate whether these raters were told or not told that AI was involved.

human_samples.population: Target-audience raters recruited the same way as the previous study for the museum-art image survey.
human_samples.recruitment: Paper states raters were recruited the same as for the previous study; exact overlap with the first or Flickr survey was not reported.
human_samples.geography: Local university recruitment implied by the previous-study method; country/region not reported for participants.
human_samples.observations: 4044 ratings for the museum-art image study; 30 museum art images, 15 from MoMA and 15 from The Met, with 5 HumorSkills captions and 5 GPT-4o captions per image. This is an observation count, not a participant count.
human_samples.detail: Demographics reported: 7 female, 3 male, 7 declined gender; 8 aged 18-25, 2 aged 25+, 7 declined age group. Image and caption order were randomized. The paper does not explicitly restate whether these raters were told or not told that AI was involved.

human_samples.population: Instagram users who wrote comments/captions on 8 popular Instagram humor captioning accounts used for the target evaluation images.
human_samples.recruitment: Organic Instagram comments; authors selected existing posts/images and comments from popular Instagram humor captioning accounts.
human_samples.observations: For the target study, the top 5 most upvoted Instagram captions/comments for each image were used as the human-written comparison condition; with 20 images, this implies 100 human-caption items, not 100 people.
human_samples.detail: Number of unique commenters, candidate comments per post, upvote totals, post dates, and account identities were not reported.

human_samples.population: Instagram users who wrote humorous comments on three popular Instagram meme pages with over 400,000 followers, used for fine-tuning data.
human_samples.recruitment: Authors extracted existing top comments from popular Instagram meme pages.
human_samples.observations: 80 humorous comments were extracted for fine-tuning; the paper says the top five comments of each image post were collected. This is an item count, not a participant count.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number of source posts, unique commenters, source account names, post dates, or whether these fine-tuning sources overlapped with evaluation sources.

human_samples.population: Instagram users whose platform upvotes determined which comments were top-ranked.
human_samples.recruitment: Organic platform voting on Instagram posts.
human_samples.observations: Platform upvotes selected the top-five human comments for the target-study human baseline and selected top comments for the 80-comment fine-tuning dataset; exact vote counts were not reported.
human_samples.detail: These platform users were not recruited study participants; their number and demographics are unknown.

judges.name: Target Instagram humor-rating survey respondents
judges.detail: Rated captions on a 1-5 funniness scale. Caption source conditions were hidden, and users were not told AI was involved. These ratings determine the main Instagram-study quantitative results.

judges.name: Flickr camera-roll humor-rating survey respondents
judges.detail: Rated HumorSkills and GPT-4o captions for 30 Flickr images. The paper reports randomized image and caption order, but does not explicitly restate condition/AI blinding for this survey.

judges.name: Museum-art humor-rating survey respondents
judges.detail: Rated HumorSkills and GPT-4o captions for 30 museum-art images. The paper reports randomized image and caption order, but does not explicitly restate condition/AI blinding for this survey.

judges.name: Instagram upvotes
judges.detail: Determined which existing human-written Instagram comments were the top-five comparison captions in the target Instagram study.

judges.name: Instagram upvotes for fine-tuning examples
judges.detail: Top-five comments from popular Instagram meme-page posts were extracted to form the 80-comment humorous-caption fine-tuning dataset.

judges.name: GPT-4o-based Gen Z humor ranking/filtering agent
judges.detail: Within HumorSkills, this agent ranks 30 generated captions by humor, relatability, and alignment with the image/narrative, filters weaker captions, and returns the top five evaluated HumorSkills captions.

judges.name: Paper authors
judges.detail: Authors provide the qualitative analysis of examples in Section 5, attributing performance to Gen Z slang/fine-tuning, narrative extrapolation, and visual detail extraction. No independent qualitative coders are reported.

judges.name: MixedLM/GLMM regression over human ratings
judges.detail: Statistical models over human ratings produced condition coefficients and p-values. The paper text describes a GLMM for ordinal ratings, while the regression tables are labeled MixedLM with REML.

generation_selection.scope: HumorSkills outputs for the Instagram, Flickr, and museum-art evaluations
generation_selection.n: 30 generated captions per image: 15 captions generated without external narratives and 15 generated with external narratives; the ranker returns the top 5.
generation_selection.detail: A GPT-4o-based Gen Z humor ranking agent selects the evaluated HumorSkills captions based on humor, relatability, and alignment with the image and narrative.

generation_selection.scope: GPT-4o baseline outputs for the Instagram, Flickr, and museum-art evaluations
generation_selection.detail: The paper reports 5 GPT-4o captions per image and describes the baseline as GPT-4o with prompt engineering/a simple humor prompt. No over-generation, filtering, or best-of-N selection is reported; the exact call structure is not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Used as the state-of-the-art VLM baseline with prompt engineering and within HumorSkills for visual detail extraction, visual humor ideation, and narrative/conflict extrapolation. No snapshot, API version, access method, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: Fine-tuned GPT-3.5
model_provenance.detail: Used as the HumorSkills caption generator. The paper says it was fine-tuned on 80 humorous Instagram comments and used in two prompts per image: one with generated narratives and one without. The exact GPT-3.5 variant/model ID is not reported.

model_provenance.name: GPT-4o-based Gen Z humor ranking agent
model_provenance.detail: Used as an LLM judge inside HumorSkills to rank and filter generated captions. The paper describes it as GPT-4o-based and fine-tuned/trained to evaluate Gen Z humor, but gives no model ID, training data details, snapshot, or inference date.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: At least six described prompt/model invocations per image: GPT-4o visual detail extraction; GPT-4o visual humor ideation; GPT-4o narrative/conflict extrapolation; fine-tuned GPT-3.5 caption generation without narratives; fine-tuned GPT-3.5 caption generation with narratives; GPT-4o-based caption ranking/filtering.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 10 generated narratives/conflicts; 15 captions without narratives plus 15 captions with narratives, for 30 generated captions; top 5 returned.
inference_budgets.detail: The paper summarizes some prompt instructions but does not provide full prompts, temperature/top-p/seed settings, max-token limits, latency, compute, or monetary cost.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: 5 captions per image were used in each survey.
inference_budgets.detail: Exact baseline prompt, number of API/model calls, whether the five captions were produced in one response or multiple responses, and decoding parameters are not reported. No extra selection or filtering is reported.

human_baselines.description: The human-written comparison condition consisted of the top 5 most upvoted Instagram captions/comments for each of the same 20 images.
human_baselines.detail: The human and AI captions were rated together by the same blinded survey respondents, but the human captions were preselected by platform upvotes from an unknown number of comments, commenters, and voters in a real Instagram context. The production and selection conditions are therefore not materially matched to the model generation conditions.

human_baselines.description: No human-written caption baseline was used; the paper states that top-ranked human captions were not compared because these images were not taken from Instagram.
human_baselines.detail: Only HumorSkills and GPT-4o captions were evaluated.

human_baselines.description: No human-written caption baseline was used; the paper states that top-ranked human captions were not compared because these images were not taken from Instagram.
human_baselines.detail: Only HumorSkills and GPT-4o captions were evaluated.

mitigations: Funniness ratings for the evaluated caption sets were newly collected from human survey respondents.
contamination_risk.basis: Evaluation inputs and comparison captions came from public sources: Instagram posts/comments, the public Flickr Image Dataset, and public MoMA/Met art images. The Flickr dataset predates GPT-4o, and many museum/Instagram items may also have been publicly available before model training, but the paper does not report post dates, model training cutoffs, model snapshots, or decontamination tests. The newly collected human ratings reduce reliance on public benchmark labels, but they do not rule out model exposure to images, memes, or captions. No held-out/private/newly created image set or memorization check is reported.

llm_judge_dependence.affected_claims: Claims about HumorSkills performance depend on evaluating the top 5 captions selected by a GPT-4o-based Gen Z ranking agent from 30 generated candidates per image. The final funniness scores themselves are human survey ratings, not LLM ratings.
llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Human raters evaluated the final selected captions in all three studies, but the paper does not report a human validation of the ranking agent’s rankings or an ablation without the LLM ranker.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The LLM judge is part of the generation/selection pipeline rather than the primary outcome evaluator. Its filtering role materially affects which HumorSkills outputs enter the human evaluation.

reporting_gaps: No GPT-4o snapshot/version, GPT-3.5 base variant, fine-tuned model IDs, access method, or inference dates are reported.
No decoding parameters are reported for any generation or ranking step: temperature, top-p, seed, max tokens, context limits, or stop criteria are absent.
Full prompts are not provided; the paper summarizes prompt intent for system components and describes the baseline as a simple prompt, but does not provide exact prompt text for all steps.

The GPT-4o baseline generation procedure is underspecified: the paper reports 5 captions per image but not the number of calls, whether captions were generated in one response, or any retry/filtering rules.
Fine-tuning details are incomplete: hyperparameters, train/validation split, exact source posts/accounts, comment dates, and data-cleaning criteria are not reported.

The training/evaluation separation between the 80-comment Instagram fine-tuning set and the target Instagram evaluation sources is not documented; overlap of accounts, posts, images, or commenters is not reported.
The GPT-4o-based Gen Z ranking agent is described as fine-tuned/trained, but its training data, validation procedure, prompt, and model ID are not reported.
Exact evaluation items are not identified: the 20 Instagram images, 30 Flickr image IDs, 15 MoMA items, and 15 Met items are not listed.
Instagram upvote counts, candidate comment-pool sizes, number of unique human commenters, and number/demographics of platform voters are not reported.
Participant overlap across the three surveys is not reported.
Completion, exclusion, and missing-rating handling are not reported; the observed rating counts are lower than the full factorial totals implied by the respondent/image/caption counts.
Blinding to AI involvement and caption condition is explicitly reported for the main Instagram survey but not explicitly restated for the Flickr and museum-art surveys.

The analysis is reported inconsistently: text describes a GLMM for ordinal ratings, while tables are labeled MixedLM with REML; non-target text p-values also differ from table P>|z| values reported as 0.000.
No contamination/decontamination analysis is reported for public Instagram, Flickr, MoMA, or Met content.
No ablation studies are reported to isolate the contribution of visual detail extraction, narrative extrapolation, fine-tuning, or the LLM ranking agent.
